source_file: The corporate social credit system in China and its transnational impact.txt
text:

hence becomes ‘softer’ and more informal but not necessarily less eﬃcient
and the scale between good and bad behaviour oﬀers more space to accom-
modate values and political rationale.
   ‘Joint Sanctions’78 are present in blacklist-models as well as local models
and ‘amplify the consequences of a particular behaviour beyond the original
context’.79 For corporations, this means that a transgression in one sector, eg,
a tax oﬀence, can have an immediate eﬀect on the question whether the cor-
poration will be able to provide its services in general.80 Some transgressions
and behaviours, which have been merely a legal risk, thus become a business
risk for the companies.81 In future assessments, the number of inspections is
adapted to the number of previous transgressions. Although this seems to be
rational, it creates the possibility that a transgression, which is not related to
for example product quality, leads to a lower score. The term ‘eﬃciency’ used
in this context ‘includes the meaning that any sanction against a company
ﬁled on the company’s credit record could trigger sanctions under other rel-
evant legislation’.82 Next to amplifying sanctions in one speciﬁc ﬁeld joint
sanctions also connect legal ﬁelds that are largely perceived as distinct in
the current common sense of transnational law.
   The surveillance strategies of the state have been described as a ‘panspec-
trum’83—as it is no longer one central authority that watches everyone sim-
ultaneously, as in a ‘panopticon’, but many diﬀerent ‘sensors’ that intrude all
aspects of social and economic life. The CSCS does not strictly separate the
individual in the workplace from the individual as a private citizen. The per-
sonal score of the company’s legal representative or a key employee can
inﬂuence the corporation’s scoring. Vice versa, the corporation’s scoring
inﬂuences the individual scoring of its key employees.84 This correlation
puts pressure on the company to monitor their employees not only in
their function as workforce but to take interest in their lives as private citi-
zens as well. Furthermore, ‘publicity’ and ‘reputation’ are pillars of the
system. While not all types of data will be publicly disclosed to everybody,85
publicity of certain social credit data is supposed to function as an additional
disciplinary tool that evokes public criticism and social pressure.86 This
